observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management require the:

• General Services Officer to:
  a. verify and validate the PPE items recorded in
  the accounting reports but not reported in the
  RPCPPE, and adjust the property reports
  accordingly; and b) maintain a complete Local
  Road Inventory and Road Map, and keep a
  complete Local Road Network Property Card for
  all roads and its components;
  b. record and label with property tags the
  deliverables of SPIDC individually, and
  prepare corresponding property cards. Also,
  issue Acknowledgement Receipt for Equipment
  (ARE) to the personnel responsible for said
  properties; and
  c. include in the physical inventory the donated
  equipment from EMB-CAR, label with property
  tags and prepare corresponding property cards.
  Also, issue Acknowledgement Receipt for
  Equipment (ARE) to the personnel responsible
  for said properties.

• Municipal Accountant to:
  a. verify and validate the items reported in the
  RPCPPE but not recorded in the accounting
  reports, and adjust the accounting reports
  accordingly; and b) keep and maintain ledger
  cards for roads and road components for every
  road network;
  b. coordinate with SPIDC on the unit costs of
  the delivered and installed properties. Thereafter,
  record in the books of accounts thru JEV No.
  accounts and provide corresponding
  depreciation per item, and prepare reflected in
  the inventory report. SLs were also maintained
  c. record in the books of accounts the donated
  equipment, prepare property ledger cards and
  provide corresponding depreciation; and
  issue corresponding ARE to person responsible,
  and prepared ledger card.

• Inventory Committee to: and
  a. conduct a complete annual physical
  inventory of all infrastructure assets including
  the road networks within the Municipality’s
  jurisdiction and prepare the corresponding
  RPCPPE and RPCLRN; b. include the properties
  from SPIDC in their physical inventory and
  determine whether still serviceable. In case
  unserviceable, include in the IIRUP, a copy of
  which shall be forwarded to the Audit Team; and
  c. include in the physical inventory as well, the
  donated equipment, label with property tags and
  prepare corresponding property cards. Also,
  issue Acknowledgement Receipt for Equipment
  (ARE) to the personnel responsible for said
  properties.

• Municipal Engineer to provide the Accountant
  and the GSO with the complete description and
  cost segregation of road components for road
  projects.

in order to come up with a reliable Local Road

statements;

c. the donated equipment are utilized for the
purpose as intended by the donor.

observations.recommendation:
1.11 We recommended and Management agreed to require the:
a. General Services Officer to: a) verify and validate the PPE items recorded in
the accounting reports but not reported in the RPCPPE, and adjust the
property reports accordingly; and b) maintain a complete Local Road
Inventory and Road Map, and keep a complete Local Road Network Property
Card for all roads and its components;
b. Municipal Accountant to: a) verify and validate the items reported in the
RPCPPE but not recorded in the accounting reports, and adjust the
accounting reports accordingly; and b) keep and maintain ledger cards for
roads and road components for every road network;
c. Inventory Committee to conduct a complete annual physical inventory of all
infrastructure assets including the road networks within the Municipality’s
jurisdiction and prepare the corresponding RPCPPE and Report of Physical
Count of Local Road Network (RPCLRN);
d. Municipal Accountant and Inventory Committee to reconcile the
discrepancies of Acquisition Cost of PPE items; and
e. Municipal Engineer to provide the Accountant and the General Services
Officer (GSO) with the complete description and cost segregation of road
components for road projects.
1.12 We further recommended and Management agreed to ensure adherence with the
accounting and reporting guidelines on the Local Road Asset Management
System prescribed by COA Circular No. 2015-008 in order to come up with a
reliable Local Road Network account balance in the financial statements.

2.12 We recommended and Management agreed to strictly comply with the guidelines
on the recognition of expenses when incurred and the corresponding liabilities.
Hence, the suggested adjusting entry:
Prior Years’ Adjustment 12,299,364.04
Accounts Payable 12,299,364.04
2.13 We further recommended and Management agreed to require the Municipal
Accountant to monitor the disbursements of the municipality and ensure that vouchers
with supporting documents of unrecorded and unpaid expenses are submitted
complete, intact and promptly to the accounting office before year-end for proper and
timely recognition of expenses and the corresponding payables.

4.7 We recommended and Management agreed to direct the Municipal Accountant
to transfer the unexpended unobligated DRRMF balances with aggregate amount
of ₱905,431.68 to the Special Trust Fund under the Trust Liabilities- DRRMF
account in accordance with the provisions of COA Circular No. 2012-002.

5.8 We recommended and Management agreed to require the Accounting Office to
verify the items with abnormal balances, identify the errors and adjust/ reclassify
accordingly. In case of over-remittance that resulted in abnormal balances,
reclassify said payables to Due to Officers and Employees and/or receivable from
suppliers.
5.9 We further recommended that only mandatory deductions and loans from the
GSIS and PagIBIG are withheld and remitted by the accounting unit. All other
personal loans of the employees are not to be accommodated by the accounting
unit as these are personal transactions and not official transaction of the
Municipality.
5.10 Management commented that the Municipality will have difficulty in implementing
the audit recommendation on the personal loans of employees because
accommodation of said loans were approved by the Sangguniang Bayan through
resolutions and covered by a MOA between the Municipality and the lending
institutions. However, Management will consider the recommendation and study
how to implement such.

6.9 We recommended and Management agreed to direct the Municipal Accountant
to take up the necessary adjustments through the suggested adjusting entry
under the Trust Fund books, as follows:
Guaranty Deposits Payable 34,879.51
Due to Other Funds – MDF 34,879.51

7.9. We recommended and Management agreed to require the Municipal Accountant
to:
a. restate the amount for prior periods in the comparative financial statements
to reflect the change in accounting policy that requires retrospective
application; and
b. adhere to the full disclosure of changes in accounting policy and proper
presentation of comparable financial statements as required in paragraph
118d and the Guidelines on the Presentation of Financial Statements under
IPSAS 1 and paragraph 33 of IPSAS 3.

8.9. We recommended and Management agreed to require the Municipal Accountant
to:
a. Coordinate with the Accountants of DOLE-CAR and DOST-CAR and
reconcile balances of the funds received from said agencies; and
b. Exert effort in locating the Memorandum of Agreement for the identified fund
transfers from DOT and DOH and submit copies to the audit team for
validation.

9.15 We recommended and Management agreed to require the Municipal
Development Council to:
a. ensure that proposed development projects are sufficiently funded and free
from location disputes;
b. carefully evaluate feasibility of approved projects, especially those of prior
years that have not been started. If not viable, revert the funds to the
unappropriated DF for re-appropriation to other priority projects; and
c. ensure that development projects are well-planned and procurement-and-
implementation ready.
9.16 We further recommended and Management agreed to require the Municipal
Engineering and Municipal Planning and Development offices to strictly monitor
the progress of works and immediately act on problems identified to facilitate
timely implementation of infrastructure projects.

10.19 We recommended and Management agreed to require the MDRRMC to:
a. evaluate feasibility of planned projects, especially those that have not been
started. If not viable, revert the funds to the unappropriated surplus for re-
appropriation to more-needed disaster-related projects;
b. ensure that proposed projects are free of problems on location/site;
c. coordinate with the MEO on how to facilitate and fast track preparation of
plans and specifications of projects; and
d. ensure timely implementation of identified programs/projects/activities to
strengthen the Municipality’s capability to respond to disaster situations.
10.20 We further recommended and Management agreed to incorporate in the
LDRRMFIP a separate caption of projects and activities to be charged to the
unexpended LDRRMF of previous years.

12.6 We reiterated our previous recommendations and Management agreed to
require the:
a. Property/ Supply Officer to verify the inventory items not included in the
RPCI and reconcile with the supply ledger cards of the Accounting Office,
and vice versa; and
b. Accounting Office to adjust inventory items with zero quantity but with SL
balances.
12.7 We further recommended and Management agreed to henceforth, require the
Property/ Supply Officer to regularly update the stock cards and reconcile with
the supply ledger cards of the Accounting Office on a regular basis.

13.7 We recommended and Management agreed to:
a. Issue demand letters to the contractors concerned for the refund of the
outstanding advances, otherwise file appropriate charges against the erring
contractors and recommend for their blacklisting; and
b. Closely monitor advances granted to contractors and immediately forfeit
the standby letter of credit, bank guarantee or surety bond in the event of
non-compliance with the terms and conditions in the grant of mobilization
fund.

14.5 We recommended and Management agreed to require the implementing offices
relative to the LT Community Business Center, TUPAD program, Livelihood
Program for PDLs, Plantation Establishment project and National Greening
Program, to facilitate completion/ implementation thereof.
14.6 We further recommended and Management agreed to require the Municipal
Environment and Natural Resources Officer to exert efforts in locating the
documents relative to the Plantation Establishment project and coordinate with
DENR on the lacking documentary requirements under the National Greening
Program, and facilitate payment of workers thereof.
14.7 We likewise recommended and Management agreed to ensure constant
monitoring on the implementation of projects and immediate return of funds to
the Source Agency, if warranted, to avoid accumulation of unutilized fund
transfers from NGAs.

15.6 We recommended and Management agreed to:
a. exert more efforts to locate the concerned NGOs/POs and issue demand
letters for the immediate submission of the Fund Utilization Report;
b. conduct proper monitoring and evaluation of the projects undertaken by the
recipient NGOs/POs to determine whether the funds were utilized for its
intended purpose; and
c. avail of legal remedies in the event of continuous non-compliance by the
POs/NGOs of the requirement on the submission of liquidation reports.

16.6 We recommended and Management agreed to require the Building Official to
prepare a Program of Works for approval of the LCE and submit to the DPWH
as prescribed under the DPWH-DILG JMC, for the efficient utilization of the 15
per cent share of the OBO.

17.13 We recommended and Management agreed to ensure compliance with the
guidelines provided under the JMC for the determination of the percentage of the
budget of the regular LGU programs and projects that may be attributed to the
GAD Budget and compliance with the five per cent of the total appropriations
allocation.
17.14 We further recommended and Management agreed to require the GAD Focal
Point System (GFPS) to support the GPB with documents such as project
proposal and the GAD AR with a summary or breakdown of the reported
program/projects as basis for the determination of gender issues to be
addressed by the GAD-related PPAs.
17.15 Furthermore, we recommended and Management agreed to ensure that all
members of the GFPS, TWG and Secretariat be capacitated on the GAD tools,
specifically on the HGDG tests, to aid in its administration.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management immediately submit the following:

an inventory report on the property and cash accountabilities of the deceased former Treasurer to include an express declaration of shortages, if any, for the audit team’s review and approval  and for purposes of claim against the estate of the former treasurer, if warranted; and
reclassify the Cash-Local Treasury account under the name of the former Treasurer to Other Receivable account once the shortage is clearly established.

We recommended that Management require the immediate liquidation and/or refund of the cash advances, and consider imposing withholding of salaries to AOs who do not comply with the required liquidation of their respective cash advances.

We recommended that management:
Avoid grant of cash advance to an officer or employee unless his previous cash advance has been liquidated;
Increase the bond of the disbursing officers to cover their maximum cash accountability; and
Consider use of bank services in payment of salaries, wages and other benefits to lessen grant of cash advances.

We recommended that the Management:
execute lease contracts with stall occupants of the 2002 Public Market Building pursuant to Municipal Ordinance No. 3-2005 with consideration of the rental rates as prescribed under the Municipal Revised Revenue Code of 2017;
request the Sangguniang Bayan to enact an ordinance for the operations and management of the 1993 Public Market Building and other real property of the Municipality for lease purposes; and
prepare journal voucher entries to recognize as income/receivables the unpaid rentals of stalls and lots occupied by private buildings.

We recommended that Management require the Accounting Office to recognize the issued inventories as expenses, and take-up the ₱1,246,301.24 balance of the Food Supplies Inventory in the books.

We further recommended that henceforth, the Accounting Office comply with the accounting standard on inventories to ensure that account balances are fairly presented in the financial statements.

We recommended that Management require:
The Property Unit/Office to maintain updated stock cards and for the Accounting Division to maintain updated supply ledger cards; and
The Property Unit/Office and the Accounting Division to reconcile the physical inventory of supplies, the stock cards and supply ledger cards on a quarterly basis.

We recommended and Management agreed to:
Require the appraisal of the lots owned by the Municipality by a licensed appraiser for purposes of the land valuation; and
Direct the Accountant to consider application of the Revaluation Model as accounting policy on the Land account of the PPE, to come up with a fair presentation of the account balance.

We recommended that Management revise the estimated life of the Infrastructure Assets to conform with the Circular and to adjust the Accumulated Depreciation of the respective accounts.

We further recommended that Management record all infrastructures constructed prior to CY 2019 to present fairly the PPE accounts of the Municipality.
We recommended that Management consider the insurance of appropriate properties of the Municipality with the GSIS against insurable risks and losses.

We recommended that Management dispose of the unserviceable properties in accordance with the Handbook on Property and Supply Management as prescribed by COA Circular No. 92-386 to prevent further deterioration of the stocked items and to derive income from sale thereof.

We recommended and Management agreed to:
Acknowledge all cash donations through the issuance of an official receipt and deposit the same with an Authorized Government Depository Bank (AGDB) of the Municipality;
Submit a Liquidation Report with supporting documents to fully account the cash donations amounting to ₱348,383.00: and
Direct the Accounting Unit to reclassify the unexpended Cash Donations under “Other Payables” account to “Trust Liabilities-DRRMF”, and henceforth, to account for and utilize cash donations in accordance with the prescribed guidelines.

We recommended that Management provide an explanation on the utilization of the BGCM for the ₱2,525,915.00 disbursements not included in the allowable PPAs and expenses under LBC No. 125.

We further recommended that management utilize funds in accordance with the purpose for which these were granted.

We recommended that Management provide an explanation on the non-creation of a special account for the BGCM.

We further recommended and Management agreed that henceforth  a Special Account in the General Fund for special purpose funds will be created to facilitate monitoring and utilization thereof.

We recommended and Management agreed to:
Submit a summary of all welfare goods procured out of the BGCM distributed by the Municipality to the various barangays for the year;
Devise and implement system of procurement, packing and distribution of welfare goods; and
Come up with standard criteria or qualification of beneficiaries to be considered in case of another wave of mass distribution of welfare goods.

We recommended that Management provide an explanation on the advance payment of more than the 50% limit provided in Memorandum Order No. 48. Further, we recommended that henceforth, Management comply with the provisions of the Memorandum.

We recommended that Management require the:
Municipal Development Council to ensure that all MDF projects partake of the nature of investment or capital outlay and carefully evaluate feasibility of the projects, especially those that have not been started. If not viable, revert the funds to the unappropriated DF for re-appropriation to other priority projects; and
Municipal Engineering to strictly monitor progress of works and facilitate timely implementation of infrastructure projects.

We further recommended and Management agreed to consider hiring more personnel or creating a Buildings and Architecture Office to be able to meet the growing workload of the MEO.

We recommended that management separate the operations budget for the MDRRMO from that of the LDRRMF, specifically the Mitigation Fund.  We further recommended that, henceforth, utilization of the LDRRMF be strictly limited to expenses as prescribed under the Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-1 of the NDRRMC, DBM and DILG dated March 25, 2013 and COA Circular No. 2012-002.
We recommended that henceforth, the guidelines on the payment of honoraria and overtime pay to personnel involved in procurement be strictly complied with to preclude audit disallowances.

We recommended and Management agreed to require the concerned BAC personnel to prepare a checklist of all required documents based on existing laws, rules and regulations including COA circulars to serve as guide in the submission of contracts and supporting documents, to ensure completeness.

We further recommended and Management agreed to submit immediately the required documents for evaluation/review by the audit team.

We recommended that in order to ensure that the goods, equipment, facilities and services included in the project are utilized exclusively for the purpose of carrying out the project considering that there is no greenhouse at the moment, LGU La Trinidad take custody of all the goods procured by the PG for safekeeping until the greenhouse is constructed. We further recommend that the LGU exert effort to compel the PG to acquire the needed project site for the construction of the greenhouse.

We recommended that the LGU review the procurement conducted by the PG and rectify any deviations from standard procedures that are acceptable under the circumstances. LGU La Trinidad shall see to it that it does not deviate from its commitment embodied in the IMA.

We recommended and Management agreed to ensure compliance with the administration of the HGDG tests for the determination of actual expenditures to be attributed to GAD budget. Further, we recommended and Management agreed to consider hiring personnel as support staff dedicated to GAD; and that all members of the GAD TWG and Secretariat be capacitated on the GAD tools, specifically on the HGDG tests, to aid in its administration.

We recommended that Management ensure compliance with the schedule of submission of the year-end financial statements and relevant reports on or before February 14 of the ensuing year pursuant to COA Circular No. 2010-001 dated March 2, 2010 to enable the audit team to conduct timely verification/review of financial statements and reports.
We further recommended that management consider imposing appropriate sanctions/penalties for continuous disregard of the deadlines in submission of required reports.

We recommended and Management agreed that taxes withheld be timely remitted to the BIR pursuant to Revenue Regulations No. 2-98 dated April 17, 1998, as amended. We further recommended and Management agreed that taxes withheld and remitted by the Municipality be correctly and properly posted in the ledgers to preclude abnormal balances in the Due to BIR account which may result in the excessive remittances to the BIR.

report the 174 unserviceable items in the IIRUP and follow disposal procedures prescribed in COA Circular No. 89-296 dated January 27, 1989; and



require the Accounting and property Units to reconcile their records regularly to establish the correct balance of each of the PPE on a fund cluster basis.

Team; and
4.7.3 Verify the nature of the ending negative balances of the account and effect
the necessary corrections/ adjustments.

5.6 We recommended that Management strictly comply with the policy of not granting
a cash advance to officials and/or employees whose previous cash advance were not
yet liquidated or not properly accounted for.

5.7 Management commented that through the Human Resource Management Office,
policies will be created for prompt and simultaneous submission of Daily Time Records
of all job order employees for possible one-time grant of cash advance for payroll to
designated disbursing officers.

6.8 We recommended that the Municipality require the individuals with long
unliquidated cash advances under accounts Due from Officers and Employees and
Other Receivables to settle or liquidate their long outstanding cash advances.

6.9 We also recommended that the Audit Team be furnished of the present office
assignments of the individuals listed in the table, supra, for referral to the Civil
Service Commission, if warranted under CSC MC No. 23, s. 2019.

7.8 We recommended that Management comply with the provisions of the NGAS
Manual, and henceforth, set up RPT and SET Receivables based on a list from the
Municipal Treasurer showing the name of taxpayers, and the amount due and
collectible for the year, to come up with an accurate and realistic account
balances. We also recommended that the tax receivable accounts be reconciled
with the tax delinquencies to come up with a consistent balance.

8.6 We recommended that Management complete the physical count of Inventories
and reconcile data with accounting records, and henceforth, to conduct such
regularly as required.

8.7 Management will comply with the recommendation and will issue an administrative
order reorganizing the Inventory Committee to reconcile data with accounting records
and conduct periodic inventory and preparation of RPCI, Further, Management will
operationalize the property division of the General Services Office as per Ordinance
No. 25-2016.

9.3 We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to effect the appropriate
adjustments on the inventory account to come up with correct balances.

10.6 We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to provide depreciation
and present the infrastructure assets in the financial statements at cost less
accumulated depreciation and any accumulated impairment loss, if any.

11.3.1.4 We recommended that Management ensure strict adherence to
the provisions of the JMC on expenditures that should be funded
out of the MDF.

11.3.2.4 We recommended that Management study the feasibility of the
projects, especially those that have not been started yet and
facilitate implementation. If not feasible, revert the funds to the
unappropriated DF for re-appropriation to more feasible priority
projects.

11.3.3.8 We recommended that Management:
11.3.3.8.1 Consider developing controls/ strategies on how to
address and prevent the encountered site issues;
11.3.3.8.2 Study controlling the number and nature of projects
proportionate to the capacity of the technical
resources and available time;
11.3.3.8.3 Study putting parameters on unimplemented prior
years projects, when to continue and when to revert to
unappropriated DF, to prevent accumulation; and
11.3.3.8.4 Fast track completion of unimplemented projects.

12.8 We recommended that the Municipal Accountant revert the unexpended
LDRRMF from CY 2010 to the unappropriated surplus of the GF; and henceforth
to do the same to prior years’ unexpended LDRRMF balances aged five-years or
more.

12.9 Management stated that the outstanding balance of the continuing appropriation for CY
2010 was obligated for as counterpart for Philippine Rural Development Project and
was disbursed during the CY 2019 resulting to zero ending balance as of December
31, 2019.

13.4 We recommended that Management direct the preparation and inclusion of the list
of projects and activities charged to the unexpended LDRRMF of previous years to
the DRRMFIP, as well as disclosures on the amount and details of the LDRRMF
unexpended balance in the Notes to the Financial Statements.

13.5 Management commented that the DRRM Officer will incorporate to the DRRMFIP the
identified Programs, Projects and Activities appropriated under the supplemental
budget/ realignments, and regularly update the LDRRMFIP.

13.6 On the other hand, the Municipal Accounting office will disclose the amount and details
of the LDRRMF unexpended balance in the Notes to Financial Statements.

14.4 We recommended that the Municipality discontinue the procurement of goods
and services from the La Trinidad Municipal Employees Multi-Purpose
Cooperative.

14.5 Management stated that there procurement of goods and services by the Municipality
from the LTMEMPC were suspended since January 1, 2020.

2.6 We recommended and Management agreed to:
2.6.1 Require the MTO to hasten the preparation of the RPCPPE for the
remaining offices;
2.6.2 The MTO to prepare the IIRUP for the unserviceable PPEs;
2.6.3 The MTO to submit to the Disposal Committee the IIRUP for their
appropriate disposition of the unserviceable PPEs and submit to
MAccO the report;
2.6.4 Require MAccO to prepare Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) for the
dropping of the property from the books of accounts and for taking
up the proceeds from the sale of the property if warranted;
2.6.5 Demand explanations from the concerned AO reasons for his failure
to present the motor vehicle during the inventory taking of property
accountability; and
2.6.6 Submit to the Audit Team the copy of Acknowledgement Receipt for
Equipment for the motor vehicle not presented.

3.8 We recommended and management agreed the following actions to be
taken:
3.8.1 Instruct the Municipal Engineer to provide the Municipal
Accountant the complete description and cost segregation of road
components for road projects;
3.8.2 The Municipal Accountant to prepare the Journal Entry Voucher to
recognize the local road network and its components; and
3.8.3 The GSO/MTO to maintain a Local Road Inventory and Road Map
and LRN property card for all roads and its components.

4.12 We recommended the MAccO to:
4.12.1 Coordinate with the MTO, to perform immediate identification and
thorough analysis/verification of all outstanding items not yet taken
up in the books of accounts in order to establish the correct balance
of the CIB-LCCA and furnish the Audit Team copies of the JEVs
taking up the adjustments;
4.12.2 Submit immediately the documents supporting the unrecorded
disbursements totalling ₱692,770.72, for review and validation by
the Audit Team; and
4.12.3 Validate the listed stale checks and verify whether replacements
were already made and prepare adjusting journal entries to take up
the appropriate stale checks to present the correct cash balance in
the financial statements.
4.12.4 Regularly prepare and submit the BRS to facilitate timely
identification of items needing adjustments; and
4.12.5 Promptly prepare the necessary JEVs to recognize valid reconciling
items to ensure accurate reporting.

5.7 We recommended that Management require the MAccO the following:
5.7.1 Identify the cause of the discrepancies and effect immediate adjustments in
the books of accounts;
5.7.2 Prepare a monthly reconciliation of reciprocal accounts and ensure that
reconciling items are resolved and/or adjusted on a timely basis; and
5.7.3 Perform constant review and appraisal of transactions to rectify errors and
omissions.

6.11 We recommended Management to:
6.11.1 assert its ownership and control over the lots by compelling the
occupants to enter into a lease contract covering the lots occupied by
them based on the rates prescribed under the Revised Revenue
Code of 2017 of the Municipality;
6.11.2 desist from issuing the business permits to the lot occupants if they
refuse to enter into lease contracts; and
6.11.3 issue closure order on the business establishments if occupants
insists on operating without the required business permit.

7.6 We recommended Management the following:
7.6.1 Execute individual lease contracts with booth occupants in the LGU
public market buildings in compliance with the guidelines
prescribed under Ordinance No. 3-2005.
7.6.2 Ordinance No. 3-2005 should be applied in suppletory manner to
the other public market buildings if no existing guidelines were
provided for their occupation.

8.3.1 We recommended and Management agreed to:
8.3.1.2 Submit the GAD AR to Department of Interior and Local
Government Regional Office within the prescribed period
as provided in JMC No. 2016-01; and
8.3.1.3 Strictly adhere to Item V of COA Circular No. 2014-001 for
the timely submission of the GAD Plan and Budget and AR
to the Audit Team.

9.5 We recommended Management that for the ensuing year, the GFPS should
take note and modify the GAD GPB for any observations and
recommendations embodied in the review certificate by the PPDO and or
the DILG to attain a more specific, measureable, attainable, realistic and
time bound GAD PAPs.

10.6 We recommended that the Municipality submit a valid justification for the
continued support or a practical solution for the achievement of the
objectives of the project “Fancy Lettuce Seedling Production”, otherwise,
discontinue the support for the project.

11.2 We recommended that the accountable officer or the person who received
the agricultural supplies, in behalf of the project proponent, “Gusaran
Lower Wangal Irrigators Association, Inc.,” from the Municipality to
immediately provide safety measures for the preservation of the potting
media materials for future use.

12.5 We recommended Management the following:
12.5.1 Submit a detailed cost estimate of the ₱300,000.00 unit cost per
greenhouse structure. The cost per item is necessary to determine
the amount to be deducted from the claim of suppliers in case of
non-delivery; and
12.5.2 Explain why the three greenhouse structures were accepted and
fully paid despite the non-delivery of some of the items ought to be
procured.

13.8 We recommended that the Municipality:
13.8.1 Immediately remit to the BIR the remaining taxes withheld totalling
₱140,016.73 in compliance with BIR RR No. 2-98 and bear/pay the
corresponding surcharges and penalties including compromise fee,
if any, out of their personal funds for their failure to remit the taxes
withheld within the prescribed period; and
13.8.2 Henceforth, comply strictly with BIR RR No. 2-98 and remit to the
BIR all taxes withheld within the ten days after the end of each
month, except for taxes withheld for December which shall be
remitted not later than January 25 of the following year, to avoid
surcharges/penalties and to enable the timely use of tax revenues to
finance the government’s various undertakings for the benefit of the
public.

observations.recommendation:
We reiterated the following prior years’ audit recommendation:


           1. Require the Inventory Committee to conduct a physical count
              of the Municipality’s supplies at least every six months and
              PPE at least annually; and


           2. Prepare the corresponding RPCI and RPCPPE and  ensure  timely
              submission of the same to  the  Municipal  Accounting  Office
              (MAccO) for reconciliation with the books of accounts, and to
              the Audit Team for verification.

We reiterated the following prior years’ audit recommendations that
         the MAccO:


           1. Coordinate with the Municipal Treasurer’s Office, to  perform
              immediate identification and  thorough  analysis/verification
              of all outstanding items not yet taken up  in  the  books  of
              accounts in order to establish the correct balance of the CIB-
              LCCA and furnish the Audit Team copies of the JEVs taking  up
              the adjustments;


           2. Submit immediately the documents  supporting  the  unrecorded
              withdrawals totaling ₱307,663.51, for review  and  validation
              by the Audit Team; and


           3. Prepare adjusting journal entries to take up the stale checks
              totaling ₱30,863.00 to present the correct  cash  balance  in
              the financial statements.


     12. We further recommended the MAccO to:


           1. Regularly prepare and  submit  the  BRS  to  facilitate  timely
              identification of items needing adjustments; and


           2. Promptly  prepare  the  necessary  JEVs  to  recognize   valid
              reconciling items to ensure accurate reporting.

We reiterated the following prior years’ recommendation that
         Management:


           1. Send demand letters to the NGOs/POs to immediately submit the
              liquidation reports and its supporting documents in
              compliance with the prescribed COA Circular Nos. 2007-001 and
              2012-001; and


           2. Closely monitor the liquidation of funds transferred to
              NGOs/POs.

We recommended the following:


           1. For the MAccO to identify the cause of the discrepancies and
              effect immediate adjustments in the books of accounts;


           2. For the MAccO to prepare a monthly reconciliation of
              reciprocal accounts and ensure that reconciling items are
              resolved and/or adjusted on a timely basis; and


           3. For the MAccO to perform constant review and appraisal of
              transactions to deter errors and omissions;

We recommended management to:


           1. Require the MSWDO and the MAO to intensify collection efforts
              of overdue accounts by issuing demand letters  to  delinquent
              borrowers or to demand payment from the co-maker of the loan,
              in case the recipient is not capable to pay;

           2.  Require  the  implementing  offices   to   conduct   regular
              monitoring of the proposed livelihood/agricultural  projects;
              and


           3. Evaluate the viability of projects prior to loan approval to
              minimize delayed payment of loans.

In view of the foregoing, we recommended that
                     management immediately reduce the CC by ₱109,051.97
                     prior to final payment to the contractor.
...

                  5.  In  view  of  the  foregoing,  we  recommended   that
                     management cause the immediate refund  of  the  excess
                     contract cost of ₱151,828.65.

We recommended and management agreed to ensure strict compliance
        with the provisions of RA No. 9184 in all its procurement
        activities.

We recommended and management agreed to promptly prepare the
         business plans for the LTVTP and the SVHR to ensure financial self-
         sufficiency and sustainability.

We recommended and management agreed to:


           1. Review all charges made to the EE and transfer  from  the  GF
              the cash  corresponding  to  those  charges,  which  are  not
              related to the operations of the enterprise to render a  more
              accurate report on its financial status;


           2. Prepare journal entries  to  correct  the  Cash  account  and
              Retained Operating Surplus of the EE; and


           3. Limit the charges against the Income derived from the
              operation of the EE only to those that relate to such
              operation.

In view of the  foregoing,  we  recommended  and  the  Municipality
         agreed to limit the utilization of the SEF to the allowed  expenses
         as prescribed by rules and regulations.

We recommended and management agreed to  ensure  strict  compliance
         with the provisions of the CSC-COA-DBM Joint  Circular  No.  1,  s.
         2017 during the planning and budgeting for the SEF.

We reiterated  our  prior  year’s  recommendation  that  Management
         consider the following:


           1. Ensure that only feasible Programs/Projects/Activities (PPAs)
              are included in the Annual Investment Plan (AIP).  PPAs  with
              problems, issues, complaints, and some other  reasons  should
              not be considered; and


           2.   Formulate/develop    specific    strategies/solutions    to
              effectively   implement   development   projects.    Consider
              formulating a timetable within which to  start  and  complete
              the unimplemented projects.


      8. We further recommended the following:


           1. Evaluate if projects which have remained  unimplemented,  due
              to  problems  encountered,  are  still  feasible;  otherwise,
              realign  their  budgetary  allocations  to   other   priority
              projects in the AIP; and


           2. Fill-up vacant positions in offices implementing
              infrastructure projects and, if still necessary, employ
              contractual personnel to assist in the preparation of the
              required project documentation.

We recommended that management fast track the implementation of the
         project in order to attain its objectives and  be  mindful  of  all
         activities to be undertaken in the project implementation.

We recommended that the municipality:


           1. Regulate the presence of waste pickers at the  SL  to  reduce
              the risk to these individuals; and


           2. Provide regular trainings on SL  to  concerned  personnel  to
              ensure that the SL is operated efficiently and effectively.

We recommended that the municipality extend financial and technical
         assistance to the barangays in order to establish and  sustain  the
         operation of the MRFs.

In view of the foregoing,  we  recommended   that   the   LGU
          accelerate/maximize the utilization of funds by adopting efficient
          and  timely  procurement  procedures  in   accordance   with   the
          guidelines of the Bank.


      10.  We  further  recommended  that  the  LGU  keep   track   of   the
          implementation of the sub-projects vis-à-vis the approved SPIP.

We recommended management to create its GAD M&E Team or expand  the
         functions  and  membership  of  the  Municipal  Project  Monitoring
         Committee to include the GAD Focal Point  System  (GFPS)  Technical
         Working Group Chair,  Human  Resource  Management  Officer,  Budget
         Officer, and representatives from  women’s  organizations  and  the
         academe pursuant to Philippine Commission on Women-  Department  of
         Budget  and  Management  and  National  Economic  and   Development
         Authority  (PCW-DBM-NEDA)   JMC   No.   2013-01   to   assess   the
         effectiveness and efficiency of its gender mainstreaming efforts.

We recommended that the GFPS annually prepare the Status Report  on
         Institutional Mechanisms for Gender Mainstreaming  and  submit  the
         same to the DILG Regional Office pursuant to Section 5.3 of JMC No.
         2013-01.

observations.recommendation:
1.6 We recommended and Management agreed to ensure the timely
preparation and submission of the annual RPCPPE to allow for proper
reconciliation of property records and for control and monitoring
purposes.

2.10.1 Coordinate with the Municipal Treasurer’s Office, to perform
immediate identification and thorough analysis/verification of all
outstanding reconciling items not yet taken up in the books of
accounts in order to establish the correct balance of the Cash in
Bank–LCCA and furnish the Audit Team copies of the Journal Entry
Vouchers taking up the adjustments;
2.10.2 Submit immediately the documents supporting the unrecorded
withdrawals, totaling ₱813,397.30, for review and validation by the
Audit Team; and
2.10.3 Prepare adjusting journal entries to take up the stale checks
totaling ₱63,309.03 to present the correct cash balance in the
financial statements.

2.11 Management assured compliance with the audit recommendations. It will also
try to reconstruct the missing vouchers and the supporting documents.

3.8 We reiterated our previous recommendation that Management require
the officials and employees concerned to immediately liquidate their long
outstanding cash advances in accordance with pertinent laws and
regulations.

3.9 We further recommended for Management to establish a policy on
monitoring of cash advances to ensure that immediate action is taken to
prevent recurrence of unliquidated cash advances.

3.10 Management replied that it will send final demand letters to the officials and
employees concerned to settle their unliquidated cash advances. If this fails,
the accounting office will deduct the same from their payroll. Further, it
strictly enforced the “No Cash Advance” policy to those with unliquidated
cash advances.

4.5 We recommended and Management agreed to analyze the occurrence of
the abnormal balance of the Due to NGAs account and effect the
necessary adjustments.

5.11 We recommended and Management agreed to:
5.11.1 Remit the unutilized/unexpended balance of PDAF to the Bureau
of Treasury in compliance with the Supreme Court decision on
PDAF;
5.11.2 Reassess the viability of PPAs, other than those funded under the
PDAF, and fast-track its implementation to warrant delivery of
benefits to the intended beneficiaries. Otherwise, return funds to
the respective source agency for additional resources that could be
put for better use on other agencies needing assistance;
5.11.3 Return the unexpended balance of completed projects to the
respective source agencies; and
5.11.4 Submit to the Audit Team the corresponding liquidation report
and other pertinent documents for post-audit and issuance of
appropriate credit notice.

6.10 We recommended and Management agreed to:
6.10.1 create a team to provide technical assistance, monitor the
utilization of fund transfers to the NGOs/POs and to inspect the
implementation of projects; and
6.10.2 send demand letters to the NGOs/POs to immediately submit the
liquidation reports and its supporting documents.

7.2.1 For the MAccO to prepare a journal entry to re-classify the
amount of ₱15,000.00 from the Due to LGUs account to Other
Payables account for proper presentation in the financial
statements;
7.2.2 For the MAccO and the Municipal Treasurer’s Office to
immediately release the amount due to the barangays entitled to
receive the share from HEDCOR;
7.2.3 For Management to return to the Source Agency the
unexpended balance of ₱69,274.43 in compliance with existing
rules and regulations; and
7.2.4 For the Municipal Treasurer to require the Barangay Treasurer
of Barangay Cruz to submit the stale check and prepare a new
check as replacement to be reported and attached to the Report
of Check Issued prepared at the period of cancellation.

8.5 We recommended and Management agreed that the Municipal
Treasurer’s Office will release the shares of the barangays from
collections on RPT, CTC and Tax on sand, gravel and other quarry
resources within five days after the end of each quarter pursuant to the
pertinent provision in the LGC for the timely implementation of
barangay programs and projects.

9.8 We recommended the following:
9.8.1 For the local chief executive to direct all personnel involved in
development projects to monitor implementation of proposed
programs and projects to ensure that funds allocated thereon are
utilized for projects in the approved annual budget;
9.8.2 Ensure that only programs, projects and activities without
problems, issues, complaints and some other reasons are
included in the annual investment plan; and
9.8.3 Formulate/develop specific strategies/solutions to effectively
implement development projects. Consider formulating a
timetable within which to start and complete the unimplemented
projects.

9.9 Management replied that due to manpower constraints as well as projects
funded by national government agencies, it could not implement all identified
PPAs under the development fund during the year. Nevertheless, it exerted
extra effort in implementing the same. For projects with issues, Management
will realign the funds to other feasible projects.

10.8 In view of the foregoing, we recommended that the Municipality limit the
utilization of the SEF to the allowed expenses as prescribed by rules and
regulations.

10.9 Management replied that, in compliance with the audit recommendation, it
will realign the funds intended for ineligible expenses included in the SEF
budget to other allowable charges.

11.9 We recommended that Management create mandatory positions or
designate employees to the LDRRMO for administration and training,
research and planning, and operations and warning and to implement
and monitor LDRRM projects and activities.

11.10 Management had created two positions under the LDRRMO and one of
which had been filled up. Further, said office hired the services of two
contractual employees to beef up the manpower requirement of the office
but when funds will allow, it will eventually create the other mandatory
positions. However, in spite of the lack of manpower, the Municipality had
been given the KALASAG award.

12.3 We recommended and Management agreed to:
12.3.1 Prepare the APP in conformity with the prescribed format
containing therein information that reflects the entire
procurement activity to ensure effective and efficient
procurement performance;
12.3.2 Employ competitive bidding as the primary mode of
procurement;
12.3.3 Support procurement of common-use supplies from commercial
sources with appropriate document evidencing that the procured
items are not available at the PS-DBM; and
12.3.4 Adopt shopping, as a mode of procurement, only in cases
provided in Section 52 of R.A. No. 9184.

13.4 We recommended that Management:
13.4.1 Collect from Polo Engineering and Construction liquidated
damages amounting to ₱201,511.44 for the delay in the
completion of the project “Construction of Solid Waste
Management Facility Sanitary Landfill Cell 3”;
13.4.2 Immediately submit to the Office of the Auditor all valid
suspension/ time extension orders or any appropriate documents
that would warrant the non-imposition of liquidated damages to
the concerned contractors, otherwise, direct the Municipal
Accountant to compute for the liquidated damages to be
collected from the contractors based on the provisions cited in
Annex E of R.A. No. 9184; and
13.4.3 Strengthen monitoring efforts to ensure that projects are
completed within the period stipulated in the contract to avoid
imposition of liquidated damages.

13.5 The Municipal Engineer replied that the delay in the completion of project
was due to a request to the Sanggunian Bayan for funding for a specific item
of work not originally included in the Program of Work. He further added that
a work suspension order was inadvertently omitted, thus, will submit to the
Audit Team all valid suspension/time extension orders.

14.4 We recommended that Management immediately require the contractor:
14.4.1 To cause the refund of the cost of the noted deficiencies
pertaining to work items which were not in accordance with the
contracted design and specifications of the project totaling
₱320,937.34 plus the cost of door closer; and
14.4.2 To institute appropriate corrective measures to rectify the noted
defects/damages and to clear all unnecessary materials and clean
all repaired rooms in the Strawberry Valley Hotel and
Restaurant Building.

14.5 We further recommended that Management:
14.5.1 Instruct the Municipal Engineers of projects to perform regular
monitoring and supervision to ensure that construction works
are accomplished in accordance with the terms of the contract;
and
14.5.2 Submit the PERT-CPM of the project “Improvement of
Strawberry Valley Hotel and Restaurant Building” for further
review.

14.6 Management replied that it will comply with the audit recommendations. With
regard to the noted deficiencies, the Municipal Engineer will validate the
findings of the COA-TAS prior to collection of refund from the contractor.

15.10 We reiterated the prior years’ audit recommendation that the SB
members fast track and prioritize the passing and approval of the
Revised Revenue Code to increase the Municipality’s revenue for the
benefit of its constituents.

15.11 Management commented that if the Revised Revenue Code will pass the 2nd
final reading on April 25, 2017, the same might be approved before the year
ends.

16.9 We strongly recommended that the SB immediately pass an ordinance
providing for the rates, fees or charges that will be collected from the
guests/customers for the services rendered by the hotel.

16.10 Management commented that it had created an interim management
committee that will recommend to the SB the appropriate fees or charges
that will be collected from the guests/customers of the hotel.

17.9 We recommended and Management agreed that it will identify and
finance PPAs related to the conduct of TseKap services to improve the
utilization of the PFP fund and ensure its optimum use.

19.5 We recommended and Management agreed that the MHO will furnish a
copy of the BS to the MAccO to enable the latter to recognize in the
books of accounts all billed PFP claims as provided in the above
guidelines as well as to consistently apply on all transactions the accrual
basis of accounting.

20.6 We recommended and Management agreed to:
20.6.1 Ensure that all its insurable property, assets and interests are
insured with the GIF administered by the GSIS in compliance
with R.A. No. 656; and
20.6.2 Ensure that adequate budget for payment of the required
insurance premiums are included in the annual performance
budget and paid to the GSIS.

21.5 We recommended and Management agreed that it will immediately
mark all its motor vehicles “FOR OFFICIAL USE ONLY” (3 inches),
under which shall be written the name of the Municipality in strict
adherence to the mandates of COA Circular 76-5.

22.6 We recommended the following:
22.6.1 For the BHS Midwife to make proper representation with the
Municipal and Barangay Government for the improvement of the
BHS facilities, such as lighting and ventilation, waiting area and
comfort rooms, in order to address the concerns of its patients;
22.6.2 For the BHS to make use of the recommended containers in
storing used sharp objects as well as provide covers for its
garbage bins;
22.6.3 For the BHS to provide locator charts in order to monitor the
whereabouts of health personnel daily;
22.6.4 For BHS personnel to do their utmost to extend a respectful,
warm and caring attitude to the patients;
22.6.5 For the Barangay to conduct a periodic review and validation of
its BHS to determine compliance with the Quality Standards
List and provide a copy of the report to the RHU for proper
augmentation;
22.6.6 For the Pico BHS to submit an explanation on the reported
donations given by patients; and
22.6.7 For the BHS Midwife to be provided with continuous trainings
on DOH-mandated courses to deliver quality care in health
service, to include programs not available in the BHS, to enable
them to at least provide basic information on said programs.

22.7 Management agreed to comply with the audit recommendations. With
regard to the explanation on the reported donations, the barangay midwife of
the Pico BHS admitted that a few generous patients offer voluntary
donations for the clinic but commented that “we always explain to them that
we are not allowed to accept donations because it is in violation of the Anti-
Graft and Corruption Law of the Civil Service.” She further commented that
it was clearly stated in one of the TV advertisement of the DOH na “Bawal
aang Donasyon”; hence, they were not accepting and encouraging voluntary
monetary donations.

Auditor’s Rejoinder
22.8 The Audit Team acknowledged that some patients were generous enough to
offer donations to the BHS most especially if they were very pleased with
the delivery of service; however, it was our view that the mere presence of a
donation box in the BHS, as mentioned by some donors, impliedly
encourages them to leave any amount of money in the box. Thus, to avoid
similar incidents, we further recommend that the barangay health personnel,
at all times, refrain from placing any donation box within the premises of
the health station.

23.9 We recommended that the Rural Health Unit create a committee to
craft a written manual comprising the operating mechanisms, policies,
procedures and standard protocols pertaining to the operations of the
LGU’s health stations.

23.10 Management assured that it will create a committee to craft the said manual
during the 3rd and 4th quarters of CY 2017.

24.11 We recommended and Management agreed to the following:
24.11.1 For equipment received from the DOH where the end-
user/beneficiary is the Barangay Health Station, the Rural
Health Unit will furnish a copy of the IRP to the Barangay
Bookkeeper for appropriate recording in the books of accounts
of the respective Barangay;
24.11.2 The Barangay will document receipt of donated asset by
executing a deed of donation duly signed by the donor and
donee, reflecting therein the type of asset being donated, the
related cost and the intent for use, among others, and will
furnish the Barangay Bookkeeper with copies thereof for
recording purposes; and
24.11.3 The Barangay will create a committee to appraise the donated
asset whose value is unspecified for proper recording in the
books of account.

25.4 We recommended that:
25.4.1 For the ensuing years, the MHO require the BHS to prepare
their respective PPMPs for their different projects, programs
and activities as required under Section 7.3.2 of the Revised
IRR of R.A. No. 9184; and
25.4.2 The Municipal Health Officer to incorporate in their
Consolidated PPMP the individual PPMP of each Barangay
Health Station.

25.5 The Municipal Health Officer commented that the MHO does not require
the BHS to submit their respective PPMPs since the former had not been
oriented with R.A. No. 9184, thus, was not aware of the whole procurement
process. Nevertheless, management will comply with the audit
recommendation.

26.4 We recommended and Management agreed that the Municipal Health
Officer will require all Barangay Midwives to maintain a Supplies
Logbook to record the receipts and issuances of supplies and materials
in accordance with Item 1.a, Volume II of the Systems and Procedures
Manual on the Management of Barangay Funds and Property.

27.8 Management replied that it will require the barangay midwives to undergo
trainings to be able to use the system.

28.5 We commend the municipal officials concerned for diligently
withholding the correct amount of taxes and for promptly remitting the
same to the BIR in compliance with BIR RR Nos. 16-2005 and 2-98,
thus, continually generating needed revenues for the government’s
various undertakings.

28.6 We encourage management to continue complying strictly with revenue
regulations on the withholding and remittance of taxes withheld for the
interest of the government.

29.3 We recommended that Management enforce the immediate settlement
of said disallowances.

29.4 Management had filed appeals on the said audit disallowances; hence, no
settlement had been made to date.
